lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market operators of drive-in motion-picture theaters that repeatedly sell vehicle or individual admission, concessions, rentals, and related outdoor entertainment services to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Indoor theaters, temporary one-off outdoor screenings without a recurring operator, film production and distribution, streaming services, dormant sites, non-operating landowners, and properties sold for redevelopment without a continuing drive-in operation.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumers generally buy admission to an outdoor, vehicle-oriented screening experience and may also buy concessions, rentals, advertising, merchandise, or special-event access. The operator controls the site experience and local pricing while paying film-rental, labor, utilities, insurance, technology, maintenance, and often land or lease costs.

a.bridge: BLS measures combined 512130 motion-picture and video exhibition rather than exact 512132 and reports that most employment is in ushers and ticket taking, other entertainment attendance, and food service, with smaller management, sales, administration, cleaning, security, and maintenance groups. I map scheduling, local marketing, customer messages, bookkeeping, demand and inventory forecasting, ticket and concession transactions, and programming support to remaining AI exposure, then discount physical parking-flow, food preparation, cleaning, maintenance, safety, projection, weather response, and tasks already handled by online tickets or POS systems. Drive-ins are likely more seasonal, owner-operated, physically dispersed, and concession-heavy than the combined population, so the broad occupation mix may overstate administrative exposure and understate owner-management work.
a.rationale: The available digital and administrative work is real but small beside physical site, traffic, projection, concession, and guest-service duties. Seasonality also concentrates labor in live operating windows where a minimum crew remains necessary.
a.change_evidence: An exact-512132 wage-weighted time study across seasonal and year-round sites, separating current POS, online-ticketing, projection, and scheduling automation from remaining hours and minimum-crew requirements, would materially change the range.
caveats: OEWS combines drive-ins with much larger indoor-theater operations.
No public source provides a drive-in-specific occupation or wage mix.
Large-chain digital workflows may not reflect small seasonal operators.

rho.bridge: Marcus demonstrates that exhibition workflows can use computerized box offices, online and app ticketing, self-service ticket and concession systems, QR ordering, digital menu systems, and productivity-oriented technology. I translate those indoor-chain capabilities to five-year implementation of exposed drive-in workflows, then discount seasonal cash flow, small owner-led teams, older or fragmented systems, outdoor connectivity, weather-driven exceptions, limited integration budgets, and the minimum on-site crew required for traffic, safety, projection, and concessions. The source likely overstates drive-in capital and systems maturity, although cloud POS and packaged marketing tools reduce the implementation gap.
rho.rationale: Packaged ticketing, marketing, scheduling, forecasting, and bookkeeping tools can be adopted without building custom systems, but seasonal economics and hands-on operations make broad production implementation less dependable than at multiplex chains.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of U.S. drive-in owners documenting current software, AI use by workflow, connectivity, vendor and integration cost, exception rates, seasonal staffing, and verified hours saved would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The implementation evidence comes from an indoor regional chain, not drive-ins.
Digitized transactions do not establish AI-enabled labor substitution.
Very small crews may save task time without being able to remove a staffed shift.

e.rationale: The exact NAICS definition consists of establishments operating drive-in theaters, so an active LMM firm usually provides a recurring external-customer service. Discounts cover dormant or intermittently revived sites, temporary pop-ups, landholding entities, nonprofit or municipal operations, weak standalone finances, related-entity fragmentation, and sites whose lease, zoning, film-booking, or real-estate structure prevents a transferable control position.
e.change_evidence: A site-and-firm census matching active seasons, admissions and concession revenue, normalized cash flow, ownership, land and lease control, zoning, related entities, and standalone transferability would materially change the share.
caveats: No public source measures eligibility within the supplied earnings band.

The supplied n is zero under a general Information Services margin bridge even though drive-ins can combine operating cash flow with valuable land and concessions; the anchor may miss economically relevant operators but must not be replaced.
Directory sites and Census establishments do not necessarily map one-to-one to firms or control positions.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell documents completed nightclub and theater sales from 2021 through 2025 and reports broad small-business seller pressure, including 30% of owners planning to sell citing retirement. DriveInMovie.com also maintains current for-sale and ownership-change items, confirming that some drive-ins transfer as operating sites. I translate these broader marketplace signals into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability, then discount the lack of an exact-drive-in denominator, the nightclub/live-theater mix, family succession, seasonal economics, zoning and land constraints, and closures or redevelopment sales that extinguish the operating service. Marketplace evidence overrepresents businesses already for sale, so it cannot be treated as an observed incidence rate.
s5.rationale: Drive-ins are transferable in principle and often owner-led, but scarce sites, real-estate value, family ownership, and redevelopment pressure mean many ownership events do not preserve a qualifying operating company.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of active 512132 sites and firms recording operating-business sales, family transfers, lease assignments, closures, reopenings, and land redevelopment, with post-transfer operating continuity, would materially change the range.
caveats: The sale benchmarks are not specific to drive-ins and mostly cover smaller discretionary-earnings businesses.
Seller intentions do not measure completed control transfers.
A site sale, family handoff, or redevelopment is not qualifying unless operating control of a continuing theater transfers.

q.bridge: Marcus reports posted ticket and concession price increases alongside ticketing, ordering, and labor-productivity technology. I infer that drive-in labor efficiencies are not mechanically passed through to patrons, then discount distributor film-rental participation, strong substitution from indoor theaters and home viewing, family-affordability expectations, seasonal promotions, software fees, land and maintenance cost, and reinvestment in screens, sound transmission, concessions, and site amenities. Indoor-chain pricing capacity may overstate drive-in retention, while geographically distinctive sites may have limited direct local competition.
q.rationale: Admission and concession prices allow initial savings retention, but a niche seasonal operator must preserve value, absorb fixed-site costs, and compete with abundant entertainment alternatives over the five-year horizon.
q.change_evidence: Drive-in cohorts linking automation to labor hours, admission and concession pricing, film rent, technology fees, attendance, promotions, capital spending, and operating surplus across multiple seasons would materially change the range.
caveats: Indoor public-chain price behavior is not directly representative of seasonal drive-ins.
No source measures pass-through of AI-enabled savings.
Retained operating savings may be consumed by weather losses, maintenance, insurance, or site reinvestment.

d5.rationale: Broader theatrical exhibition shows mixed recovery: 2025 North American box office remained well below 2019 even as consumer and release-slate indicators improved. The drive-in directory reports about 330 active U.S. locations and documents both reopenings and closures, consistent with a durable niche but fragile sites. I expect current drive-in demand to decline moderately over five years because of seasonality, weather, land conversion, aging facilities, film supply, streaming, and a limited customer catchment, while nostalgia, family use, special events, alternative content, and occasional reopenings provide upside.
d5.change_evidence: A verified annual panel of drive-in admissions, deflated ticket and concession sales, operating days, openings, reopenings, closures, weather interruptions, special events, and local population by site would materially change the range.
caveats: No public national series measures drive-in admissions or constant-quality quantity.
The directory is enthusiast-maintained and its approximately 330 locations may not match Census establishments or firms.
Broader indoor-theater demand and release data may not translate to seasonal outdoor venues.

o.rationale: A drive-in's continuing service is inseparable from an operated outdoor site, projection rights and equipment, traffic and parking control, safety, sound delivery, guest support, and usually concessions. Software can self-serve ticketing, marketing, ordering, and scheduling but cannot supply the physical venue experience. Discounts cover quantity eliminated by home viewing, replaced by temporary pop-up screenings or venue-only rentals, or self-served to the point that the original operating model is no longer required; the larger streaming-related volume effect is kept in d5 to avoid double counting.
o.change_evidence: Site-level service maps and consumer switching data separating lost admissions from self-served transactions, together with evidence on autonomous or third-party pop-up operating models, would materially change the share.
caveats: No source directly measures operator-required share for drive-ins.
The physical-site requirement preserves operator need but does not preserve demand volume.
A temporary outdoor exhibitor could substitute for some special-event demand without acquiring a traditional drive-in.

narrative.executive_view: Drive-ins retain a hard-to-software-away physical experience, but their remaining AI labor surface is modest and implementation is constrained by seasonal, owner-operated economics. The supplied dataset finds no firms in the earnings band, and no public evidence supports overriding that anchor.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can support local marketing, customer messages, scheduling, bookkeeping, demand and concession forecasting, pricing analysis, inventory, and programming research. Traffic control, projection accountability, food preparation, cleaning, maintenance, safety, weather response, and live guest recovery remain physical, and small crews limit removable shifts.
narrative.value_capture: Posted admissions and concession prices can retain initial efficiencies, but film rent, seasonal promotions, entertainment substitution, software costs, weather losses, land expense, and facility reinvestment reduce durable capture.
narrative.firm_availability: Active drive-ins fit the recurring-service lens and some operating sites change hands, but the population is small, ownership and land are intertwined, and redevelopment or closure often destroys rather than transfers the operation. The frozen n of zero makes the supplied LMM opportunity absent despite uncertainty in the margin bridge.
narrative.demand_durability: The format remains a distinctive family and community experience, with a current national directory footprint, but lacks a reliable admissions series and faces seasonality, weather, aging assets, streaming, film-slate volatility, and land-conversion pressure.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the absence of drive-in-specific occupation and adoption data, the zero margin-derived LMM count, sparse transfer evidence, enthusiast-directory measurement, site and land entanglement, weather, season length, and use of indoor-theater demand evidence as context.
narrative.principal_driver: A distinctive physical venue keeps most surviving demand operator-required and supports repeat admissions and concession sales.
narrative.principal_weakness: The frozen earnings-band count is zero, while seasonal site economics and land pressure sharply constrain a transferable operating-firm pool.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual
sources.supports: Exact 512132 scope as establishments primarily engaged in operating drive-in motion-picture theaters.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Motion Picture and Video Exhibition
sources.supports: Combined 512130 occupation employment shares and wages for guest service, food service, management, sales, administration, cleaning, security, and maintenance.

sources.title: The Marcus Corporation 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Theater ticketing and ordering technology, self-service systems, labor-productivity intent, attendance, pricing, alternate content, and operating constraints.

sources.title: AMC Entertainment Holdings 2025 Form 10-K/A
sources.supports: Broader North American theatrical box-office recovery context, including the 2025, 2024, and 2019 levels.

sources.title: Strength of Theatrical Exhibition: December 2025 Update
sources.supports: Broader moviegoing incidence and frequency, release-slate indicators, theater reinvestment, and consumer demand for experiential attributes.

sources.title: BizBuySell 2025 Fourth Quarter Insight Report
sources.supports: Broad small-business sale activity and seller motives, including retirement pressure.

sources.title: Nightclub and Theater Business Valuation Benchmarks
sources.supports: Observed sales and financial benchmarks for nightclub and theater businesses reported to BizBuySell from 2021 through 2025.

sources.title: Drive-In Theaters Located in the United States
sources.supports: Maintained directory reporting about 330 operating U.S. drive-ins, along with closures and reopenings in the current footprint.
